coredata.dc:title: Bringing multiscale dot-sheet heterostructured assembly into lignin valorization for efficient water desalination and zinc-ion hybrid capacitors

coredata.dc:description: The notion of biomass valorization has been widely adopted to alleviate low-carbon-emission anxieties with the attributes in energy-environmental sustainability via groping multiscale-structured composite, yet still suffering from the dilemma of lignin upcycling. In this context, we propose a novel dot-sheet-assembled heterostructure featuring all lignin-derived bifunctional carbon composites (Co-N-C@CDs) that includes porous Co, N co-modified nanosheets and solvent-extracted carbon quantum dots. The photo-physical properties of lignin-based CDs are refined into multicolor green- and red-emissive fractions (GCDs and RCDs) via a facile biphasic fractionation. Owing to the high-graphite and conductivity, the red-emitting RCDs are installed with lamellar Co-N-C to favor the enlarged interlayer spacing, fast ionic intercalation-transfer and reversible chemical adsorption, thereby resulting in high-performance capacitive deionization (CDI) and zinc-ion hybrid capacitors (ZIHCs). While assembling the CDI device, the Co-N-C@CDs possess a maximum adsorption capacity of 33.64 mg g−1 NaCl in water desalination with stable restorable behavior. As the cathode of ZIHCs, it delivers an operating voltage of 2.0 V and superior energy density of 209.72 Wh kg−1, posing a long-term cycling durability at high current rates. Detailed hierarchical structure readily enables the large specific surface area, excellent percolating porosity, intercalated hydrogen bonds and redox active sites, which are conducive to not only expedite ionic infiltration and charge reaction kinetics but also reduce the energy barriers of aqueous ion adsorption. The multiscale 0D-2D supramolecular assembly breaks the frontier of lignin valorization to connecting energy-environment functions, thereby meeting the needs of carbon–neutral society.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) A brief flowchart of deriving fluorescent GCDs and RCDs and lamellar Co-N-C from technical lignin as well as their combination to assemble the 0D-2D supramolecular dot-sheet heterostructured composites using RCDs and Co-N-C. (b) The weight-average (M w) and number-average (M n) molecular weights and of lignin. (c) Quantitative functional groups (mmol/g) of hydroxyl (−OH) and carboxyl (−COOH) groups in lignin. CS-OH and CG-OH represent the content of phenolic hydroxyls in condensed syringyl and guaiacyl units, respectively. Total P-OH: the total content of phenolic hydroxyls. Fundamental substructures of lignin including (d) side-chain δC/δH 50–90/2.5–6.0 and (e) aromatic regions δC/δH 100–130/6.2–7.6 in the two-dimensional heteronuclear single quantum coherence nuclear magnetic resonance (2D HSQC NMR) spectrum. (f) Existent substructures of lignin identified in the 2D HSQC NMR spectra including β-aryl ether (A, β-O-4′ linkages with a free –OH at the γ-carbon), Cα-oxidized β-O-4′substructures (A′), resinol (B, formed by β–β′, α-O-γ′, and γ-O-α′linkages), phenylcoumaran (C, formed by β-5′and α-O-4′linkages), syringyl (S), guaiacyl (G), p-hydroxyphenyl (H), ferulate (FA), and free p-coumaric acid (pCA) units. Fluorescence emission spectra of (g) GCDs and (h) RCDs excited at different wavelengths. (i) Facilely modulating the red-shifted behavior of RCDs with potential multicolor emission (green, yellow, orange and red) via tuning the solid-to-liquid ratio (RCDs/CH2Cl2, mg mL−1). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and nanoparticle size distributions of the (j) GCDs and (k) RCDs. (l) Plots of UV–vis DRS spectra to estimate band-gap energies (E g) via Kubelka-Munk formula for the selected RCDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Full survey X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of (a) GCDs and (b) RCDs. High-resolution XPS of (c) C 1s, (d) O 1s, (e) N 1s and (f) S 2p, involving the surface-state of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and sulfur species in the RCDs respectively. (g) Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRP) decay curves (λ ex = 400 nm) of ECDs (i.e., initial CDs via ethanol-solvothermal processing of lignin prior to CH2Cl2/H2O extraction), GCDs and RCDs. (h) The relative fluorescence emission intensities of GCDs upon adding various kinds of metal-ions. The changes in fluorescent intensity upon adding Fe3+ concentrations of 0−300 μM (i) and the corresponding linear fitting plot (j) of (F0-F)/F0 vs. Fe3+ concentration. Fluorescent emission spectra upon adding Ag+ contents of 0−400 μM (k) and the corresponding linear fitting plot (l) of (F0-F)/F0 vs. Ag+ concentration.

Morphological and structural characterizations of 0D-2D architected Co-N-C@CDs composites using RCDs as the self-reinforcing additives. (a) SEM images of Co-N-C@CDs showing the hierarchical and interconnected pores. (b) TEM, (c) HRTEM and elemental mapping images of Co-N-C@CDs. (d) Full survey XPS, and the high-resolution (e) C 1s, (f) Co 2p, (g) N 1s, (h) O 1s and (i) S 2p of Co-N-C@CDs. (j) Raman spectra. (k) Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area and pore volume distribution. (l) The comparison of BET surface area between Co-N-C and Co-N-C@CDs.

Electrochemical performance and kinetics of Co-N-C@CDs installed supercapacitors using a three-electrode setup in 6 M KOH. (a) Cyclic voltammetry (CV), (b) galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD), (c) electrochemical impedance spectrometry (EIS), (d) rate performance at various current densities from 1 to 10 A/g, and (e) cycling performances at the current densities of 20 A/g (the inset images represent the first and last 10 cycles of the GCD curves) of the Co-N-C@CDs. (f) Plots showing the relationship between Zʹ and ω −1/2 in the low-frequency region. (g) CV profile representing the capacitive contribution (blue) to charge storage for the Co-N-C@CDs at a scan rate of 80 mV/s. (h) Normalized ratio of the capacitive to diffusion contributions for the Co-N-C@CDs at a varying scan rate of 5 to 200 mV/s. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Water desalination performance. (a) NaCl adsorption experimental setup using a capacitive deionization (CDI) system. (b) CV curves of Co-N-C@CDs at a sweep rate of 5 − 100 mV/s. (c) GCD curve at various current density. (d) EIS pattern. (e) Salt adsorption capacity (SAC) of NaCl solution. (f) The conductivity change within one adsorption–desorption cycle. (g) SAC values in varying initial salt concentrations from 100 to 1000 mg L−1 at different operating voltages. (h) Ragone plot of linking SAC and salt adsorption rate (SAR). (i) Comparing the SAC results from this work with other reports. (j) Schematic showing the potential advantages of Co-N-C@RCDs in CDI cell.

Electrochemical performance of zinc-ion hybrid capacitors (ZIHCs). (a) Schematic illustration of fabricating 0D-2D hydrogen-bonding supramolecular-structured assembly of Co-N-C@CDs from upvaluing lignin-metal coordination into Co-N-C with the highly dispersed RCDs. The energy storage mechanism of Co-N-C@CDs in ZIHCs. (b) EIS profiles of Co-N-C@CDs//Zn coin cell. (c) CV and (d) GCD curves in different voltage windows. (e) CV curves at different sweep rates. (f) GCD profiles at different current density. (g) Normalized contribution ratio of capacitive and diffusion-controlled capacity of Co-N-C@CDs electrode at various scan rates. (h) Specific capacitances at current densities ranging from 0.5 to 10 A g−1. (i) Long-term cycling performance (the inset images corresponds the first and last 10 cycles of the GCD curves) (j) Energy and power density plots of the Co-N-C@CDs//Zn device in comparison to other reported ZIHCs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The notion of biomass valorization has been widely adopted to alleviate low-carbon-emission anxieties with the attributes in energy-environmental sustainability via groping multiscale-structured composite, yet still suffering from the dilemma of lignin upcycling. In this context, we propose a novel dot-sheet-assembled heterostructure featuring all lignin-derived bifunctional carbon composites (Co-N-C@CDs) that includes porous Co, N co-modified nanosheets and solvent-extracted carbon quantum dots. The photo-physical properties of lignin-based CDs are refined into multicolor green- and red-emissive fractions (GCDs and RCDs) via a facile biphasic fractionation. Owing to the high-graphite and conductivity, the red-emitting RCDs are installed with lamellar Co-N-C to favor the enlarged interlayer spacing, fast ionic intercalation-transfer and reversible chemical adsorption, thereby resulting in high-performance capacitive deionization (CDI) and zinc-ion hybrid capacitors (ZIHCs). While assembling the CDI device, the Co-N-C@CDs possess a maximum adsorption capacity of 33.64 mg g−1 NaCl in water desalination with stable restorable behavior. As the cathode of ZIHCs, it delivers an operating voltage of 2.0 V and superior energy density of 209.72 Wh kg−1, posing a long-term cycling durability at high current rates. Detailed hierarchical structure readily enables the large specific surface area, excellent percolating porosity, intercalated hydrogen bonds and redox active sites, which are conducive to not only expedite ionic infiltration and charge reaction kinetics but also reduce the energy barriers of aqueous ion adsorption. The multiscale 0D-2D supramolecular assembly breaks the frontier of lignin valorization to connecting energy-environment functions, thereby meeting the needs of carbon–neutral society.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the unceasing demand of energy and environment securities in anthropic daily life, the exhausted reserves of petroleum with the pollutant discharge lead to a global quandary about balancing energy conversion and water purification [1–3]. It thus is witnessed that an urgent mission to explore renewable biomass technologies with the prospect of sustainable development. As the easily available and abundant lignocellulose in nature, its main building blocks (i.e., cellulose, hemicelluloses and lignin) have tremendous potential to serve as the diverse substitutes for petrochemical materials and energies [4]. As a massive, renewable and low-cost aromatic biopolymer, lignin accounting for ∼ 30 % of organic carbon in the biosphere demonstrates the practical promise to creating value-added fuels, arenes and composites [5], hence lowering reliance on fossil fuel supplies [6]. It also features unique bioactivities or functions that are superior to carbohydrates [7]. Over the past few decades, the ascent of lignin-first techniques that mainly deal with the lignin upvaluing is generally accepted to support the technical and economic feasibility of lignocellulose waste-free biorefining [8–10].

Lignin that primarily exists in the plant cell wall is composed of guaiacyl (G), syringyl (S) and p-hydroxyphenyl (H) units [11], which are interconnected with phenyl-ether and carbon–carbon linkages [12]. The heterogeneous molecular features of lignin render its tailored amphiphilicity, unique structure and reactivity, thereby endowing it with multifunctional applications (e.g., luminescence, environmental and energy engineering) by task-specific chemical modification [13–16]. Lignin-derived nanocarbons, such as 0D carbon quantum dots (CDs), 1D fiber, 2D nanosheets, and 3D hierarchically porous carbon or aerogel have been dedicated to reinforce the pivotal role in electrochemical systems, broadening the availability of energy and environmental sustainability [17–20]. Apart from this, the distinct aromatic conjugated structure and high carbon content (∼60%) of lignin have demonstrated to function as an ideal precursor in the preparation of CDs with excellent photo-electronic transmission performance [21]. Unfortunately, the restricted adjustability of lignin chemistry not only retards the optimization of its microstructure and interface properties, but also greatly limit the value-added utilization of lignin [5,22]. In this vein, the immediate need is to seek a facile approach for rationally steering multiscale lignin-based composites with electrochemical functionality. With the experience of multiscale-structured assembly, a composite with reduced charge transfer resistance that is consisted of 0D MoS2 nanodots and 3D MoS2 nanoflowers was prepared on a porous reduced graphene oxide (p-rGO) by a two-step method [23], providing more active sites to enhance the activity of (0D/3D) MoS2/p-rGO in the hydrogen evolution reaction. Zhang et al. prepared a new type of 0D-3D layered porous carbon electrode through selective protection, pyrolysis and alkalization containing well-dispersed Na2Ti3O7 nanoparticles with a hollow red-blood-cell like structure, leading to the high specific capacitance and rapid ion/electron diffusion dynamics [24]. Similar to the heterostructure, a multiscale-assembled supramolecular structure of all lignin-based hybrid material shoulder the duty of broadening its practical functions into energy-environmental systems such as capacitive deionization-powered water desalination (CDI) and zinc-ion hybrid capacitors (ZIHCs) [17–20].

Herein, a facile biphasic fractionation was adopted to extract the lignin-based CDs into multicolor green- and red-emissive fractions (GCDs and RCDs), leaving the residual oxidized-lignin to prepare porous biochar. Inspired from customizing the multiscale-ordered microstructure of electrode [25], the goal of this work is to strengthen hierarchical ionic channel and charge transport dynamics, resulting in the ability to rapidly store and migrate the ion/electron for multifunctional water desalination and zinc-ion hybrid capacitors. Along this line, we innovatively fabricated an all lignin-based 0D-2D heterojunction (Co-N-C@CDs) that features lamellar Co, N co-doped carbon nanosheets with highly dispersed red-emitting RCDs. It is emphasized that excellent electron donor–acceptor characteristics of the highly graphitized RCDs is favorable to the electron transport. Moreover, the stably hydrophilic RCDs improves the infiltration of aqueous electrolyte (e.g., NaCl, KOH and ZnSO4) enabling the ionic diffusion and/or storage. The intercalation of lignin-derived CDs into 2D lamellar nanosheets formed a hierarchically porous, flower-like 0D-2D supramolecular heterostructure via the weak interactions (e.g., hydrogen bonding, π-π interaction, and electrostatic attraction), building the abundant ionic transfer micro-channels with accessible redox active sites. Fortunately, the Co-N-C@CDs installed CDI device achieves excellent water desalination with high salt adsorption capacity (SAC, 33.64 mg g−1), fast salt adsorption rate (SAR, 4.3 mg g−1 min−1), and fully reversible performance. On the other hand, a self-made ZIHCs coin cell (Co-N-C@CDs//Zn) delivers a high energy density of 209 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 500 W kg−1, superior long-term lifespan (85.37% of capacitive retention) and coulombic efficiency (∼100%). Hence the novel all-lignin heterostructure not only opens an avenue to optimize biomass materials with extremely wide prospect in multifunctional energy-water engineering, but also enlightens the high-performance feasible waste-free biorefining techniques.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structures and properties of lignin and its derivate carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: As a plentiful aromatic biopolymer in nature, lignin valorization suffers from its intrinsic heterogeneity. Encouragingly, the specific phenolic substructures (i.e., G, S and H) and functional groups of lignin (e.g., −OH, −COOH and − OCH3) offer the opportunities to tailoring the carbon framework (sp 2/sp 3 hybridization) and surface decoration. As depicted in Fig. 1 a, we thus developed a successive lignin valorization to prepare the multicolor CDs and lamellar Co-N-C nanosheets, as well as, their innovative coalescent of Co-N-C@CDs. Through a mild acidolysis pretreatment, the oxidized lignin with N, S dopants can be divided into luminescent CDs and laminated Co-N-C nanosheets via ethanol-solvothermal and calcination respectively. The photo-physicochemical properties of lignin-based CDs is refined into multicolor green- and red-emissive fractions (GCDs and RCDs) via a facile biphasic fractionation. At the end, the nanoscale red-emitting RCDs with high graphite and conductivity are inserted into the 2D Co-N-C nanosheets to fabricate multiscale assembly via supramolecular week interactions (e.g., hydrogen bonding and π-π accretion) for water desalination and Zn-ion storage.

Deciphering the technical lignin with well-defined structure is essential not only to unravel the potential structure–property interplays, but also to forecast the task-specific functionality in energy-environmental materials. Generally, the molecular weight and functional groups of lignin largely affect the physicochemical properties of its derivate products [26]. Fig. 1b shows the weight- and number-average molecular weight (M w 2580 g/mol and M n 690 g/mol) of lignin with a polydispersity index of 3.74. This indicates a wide divergence of alkali lignin macromolecules, which holds the direct relationship with their oligomer distribution and derivatives. A moderate acidic pretreatment not only reduces the lignin heterogeneity but also enhances its surface functionalization at the molecular level. Taking advantage of quantitative 31P NMR technique, the lignin molecules contains 2.4493 mmol/g of phenolic hydroxyls (total P-OH), 1.712 mmol/g of aliphatic OH, and 1.3376 mmol/g of COOH respectively (Fig. 1c). The dominant species of phenolic hydroxyls is guaiacyl OH (G-OH, 1.0132 mmol/g) followed by the syringyl OH (S-OH, 0.5819 mmol/g) with a small quantity of condensed motifs (0.2112 and 0.1532 mmol/g of CG-OH and CS-OH respectively). Moreover, a relatively low content of p-hydroxyphenyl OH (H-OH, 0.4898 mmol/g) is also gauged, conforming to the identities of typical lignin in the grass family. It is noteworthy that the presence of oxygen-containing groups in lignin is conducive to in-situ introduce into subsequent carbons such as CDs and Co-N-C, generating active spots (e.g., intrinsic defects and oxygen-excited vacancies) to enhance their surface functionality. The 2D HSQC NMR technique is applied to acquire the detailed information on lignin structure, such as representative subunits and cross-linked bonds. The aliphatic region between δC/δH 50 − 90/2.5 − 6.0 shows the branching chains of lignin with C−C and aryl-ether linkages (Fig. 1d). In addition to the β − β′ (B, resinol) and β − 5′ (C, phenylcoumaran) bonds, the lignin fragments contain plentiful β-aryl ether substructures (β-O-4′, A). It has been confirmed that the crystalline structure of lignin-derived carbon has a good linear relationship with the β-O-4′ bonds of lignin [27]. In this context, the molecular substructures of lignin is referential to comprehend the properties of their luminescent CDs and biochar. The residual carbohydrates in the side-chain of lignin can also influence the disordered structure and sp 3 hybridization by localizing the C-H correlations in the β-D-xylopyranoside in the aliphatic domain. As can be seen from Fig. 1e, the cross-linked signals in the aromatic area ranging from δC/δH 100 − 130/6.3 − 7.5 disclose the basic phenylpropanoidal substructures of lignin. The corresponding assignments of the 1H–13C cross signals for the cardinal lignin motifs are shown in Fig. 1f. The cross-signals of S, G, H, ferulate (FA) and p-coumaric acid (pCA) can be easily localized. Aromatic lignin hydrocarbons with a specific S/G ratio have a tendency to interact with methoxyl groups, which is conducive to the formation of structural defects at the edge of lignin via solvothermal demethoxylation. The acylation of C-γ from lignin side-chain refers to the pCA signals [28]. It is accepted that the manipulation of the species and content of − OH and/or − COOH in lignin subunits, such as S/G/H and their condensed moieties, has a direct correlation with manipulating the basic carbon, defect structures and functional species.

Substantial endeavors have been dedicated to improving the luminescence properties of biomass-based CDs that represent a classic yet fast-growing area in photoelectric research [29,30]. It is acknowledged that the acidolysis pretreatment is an effective tactic to boost the lignin reactivity with additional carboxyl and heteroatom functional groups. The oxidized lignin fragments containing aliphatic −OH and −COOH groups offer a beneficial circumstance to enhance the fluorescence of CDs by attaching oligomers onto their surface via a mechanism of molecular-state luminescence. Moreover, the facile purification of lignin-based CDs is of importance to refine the multicolor properties and luminescence mechanism. We adopted alkali lignin as the carbon source and mild organic acid (2,4-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid) as N,S dopant via an ethanol-mediated solvothermal treatment to prepare ECDs. The multicolor GCDs and RCDs can be easily extracted through a biphasic fractionation in CH2Cl2/H2O system. As shown in Fig. S1a-c, the excitonic absorption peaks at approximately 280, 282 and 285 nm are observed in the UV–Vis spectra of the ECDs, GCDs and RCDs. This phenomenon is commonly triggered by the migration of the conjugated C−O/C−C bond with sp 2 hybridized carbon core through π-π* interactions, similar to the characteristics of top-down preparation of CDs in the UV region [31]. As shown in Fig. 1g-h, the optimal excitation/emission peaks are located in the range of 320−440 nm/478−500 nm (GCDs) and 556−665 nm/670−770 nm (RCDs), which exhibit the bright green and red light-emitting under UV-irradiation respectively. With the increased proportion of RCDs to CH2Cl2 (mg mL−1), the emissive wavelength is easily tuned from 518 to 680 nm (Fig. 1i and S2). It is possible that the CH2Cl2 phase is conducive to extract CDs with oxygen-containing groups (e.g., COOH, OH, and OCH3) and nitrogen species. Besides, the solvent polarity triggers a shift in the excitation-state energy by the interaction between solvent dipoles with fluorophore featuring the extended relaxation. TEM results disclose the uniformly dispersed quasi-spherical nanoparticles in the as-prepared GCDs and RCDs (Fig. 1j-k). A lattice spacing of ∼0.28 nm corresponding to the (020) planes of graphitic carbon is determined to RCDs (Fig. S1d). As shown in the insets, the average lateral sizes of GCDs and RCDs are approximately 1.4 and 8.01 nm respectively. The emission wavelength undergoes a red-shift with increasing particle size due to the quantum confinement delocalization of π electrons, resulting in a decrease in band-gap of RCDs (1.5 ev, Fig. 1l) [29]. In addition, the luminescence of CDs can be affected by heteroatom doping and surface modification, which control the defect structure and energy levels. Apart from that, the sp 2/sp 3 hybridized domains that incorporate the hybridizing sp 3 carbons (lignin aliphatic side-chain) into the backbone of inherent sp 2 substructures (aromatic conjugated lignin motifs) also affect the PL performance, wherein the increased ratio of internal sp 2 graphitic structure favors a red-shift of CDs with similar nanosize [32]. From Fig. S1e, the RCDs have a higher positive zeta potential (14.83 mV) than that of negative GCDs (−5.2 mV), which is due to the differentia of N, S, O functional groups.

The full-survey X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of GCDs and RCDs are shown in Fig. 2 a-b. The relative N content of RCDs (2.77%) is higher than that of GCDs (0.04 %). It has been found that N doping can interfere the surrounding electronic environment and create a new radiation recombination path [33]. The typical peaks of C 1s (∼284.8 eV), N 1s (∼392.3 eV), O 1s (∼532.9 eV), and S 2p (∼156.1 eV) are located in the selected GCDs (Fig. 2a) and RCDs (Fig. 2b). In Fig. 2c, the high-resolution C 1s pattern of RCDs can be fitted into three peaks located at 284.7 eV (C=C/C–C), 286.1 eV (C−O), and 288.7 eV (C=O). The characteristic signals for C=O, C−OH, and C-O-C are also distributed at ∼531.8, 533.1, and 534.2 eV, respectively (Fig. 2d), demonstrating that the oxygen-containing groups are doped onto the interface of the as-synthesized RCDs [34]. The N 1s spectrum can be separated into three peaks that correspond to the chemical states of oxidized N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N (Fig. 2e). The oxidized-N and graphitic-N doping belongs to sp 2 hybridization while pyrrolic N deals with sp 3 hybridization, which make a contribution to improving the properties such as biocompatibility and photostability [35]. From the S 2p of Fig. 2f, the four peaks at approximately 163.7, 166.5, 168.5, and 169.6 eV can be attributed to the existence of C-S-C covalent bonds and C-SO x -C (x = 2,3,4) sulfone bridges [29]. The N and S species are successfully co-grafted onto the surface of RCDs. Owing to the presence of polar functional groups, the fluorescence of the N, S co-doped CDs is highly stable in aqueous medium [36]. Time-resolved luminescence is a frequently-used method for investigating the mechanisms of energy and/or charge transfer between functional groups or molecular interactions [37]. Fig. 2g displays the PL decays of lignin-based CDs, where the average PL lifetimes are calculated from a bi-exponential fitting. Their lifetimes gradually decrease with red-shift of PL wavelength, indicating an influence of the core states on the radiative lifespan. In Fig. S1f, the chromatic coordinates are plotted to describe the PL characteristics of GCDs (0.21, 0.33) and RCDs (0.72, 0.28), demonstrating the tunable luminescence of lignin-based CDs via solvent extraction.

Importantly, the quality of ambient water can be monitored by using GCDs as nanoprobes for the selective fluorescence recognition of heavy metal-ions at the concentration of 1000 μM. As depicted in Fig. 2h, the fluorescence change under a UV lamp with a wavelength of 365 nm indicates that the GCDs possess a high selectivity for Fe3+ and Ag+ via the normalized PL intensity. To confirm that the dose-dependent response behavior, the PL quenching titration was performed using ion concentrations with the range of 0−400 μM. The weakening of the PL intensity of GCDs depends on the gradually increased ion-dose, while the response interval and sensitivity vary with the color. The linear fitting intervals of Fe3+ can be easily divided into low and high concentration with a boundary of 90 μM and the limit of detection (LOD) of 1.77 μM, exhibiting the calculated coefficient R2 of 0.9974 and 0.9828 respectively (Fig. 2i-j). The PL intensities decline as the concentration of Ag+ increases in the separated functional regions of 0−80 μM (linear R2 = 0.9903) and 150–400 μM (exponential R2 = 0.9674) (Fig. 2k-l), with the LOD of 6.52 μM. The selective PL quenching of GCDs is mainly provoked by the reset of functional groups containing ionized Fe3+ and/or Ag+ on their surface. In specific, the surface functional groups of GCDs hold the unique deceptive ability of metal-ions with controllable ion-radius and surface-charge, forming stable complex and/or coordination with heteroatom-doped groups (e. g., O, N, and S) [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The 0D-2D architected assembly of Co-N-C@CDs heterojunction
ce:section.ce:para: A rational design of unique electrode structure with rapid charge-migration kinetics and electron/ion transfer is of great concern to boost the electrochemical activity. Hence we proposed a novel all lignin-derived dot-sheet heterojunction using RCDs and Co-N-C as the 0D additive and 2D carbon nanosheet matrix, respectively. The highly crystallize and graphitized structure, abundant surface groups, and nano-sized nature of RCDs can not only provide electroactive sites, but also facilitate the electron diffusion with low-tortuosity route. Meanwhile, the excellent electron-acceptor characteristic of RCDs is also conducive to charge transfer and reservoir. Apart from that, the lamellar Co-N-C can be assembled with RCDs to fabricate a continuous and conductive supramolecular network via weak interaction such as hydrogen bonding, π-π stacking, and electrostatic attraction, ensuring hierarchical conductive structure, ionic transport microchannel, redox sites and mechanical durability. As presented in Fig. S3 a-c, the 2D nanosheet structure of Co-N-C is clearly observed with the uniform distribution of Co, N, C, O and S elements. The as-prepared Co-N-C@CDs exhibit a flower-like architecture with the hierarchical and interconnected pores assembling by nanosheets (Fig. 3a). From Fig. 3b, it is easily witnessed that the CDs are heterogeneously distributed to the interfaces and/or intercalation of lamellar-structured anisotropic Co-N-C, which is favor to augment the interlayer spacing, ionic microchannels within multiscale dot-sheet heterostructure capable of reducing ion-adsorption energy barrier and expediting charge reaction kinetics. The lattice fringes with d-spacings of 0.21, 0.28 and 0.46 nm refers to the graphitized RCDs, (022) [39] and (111) [40] planes of Co3O4 respectively (Fig. 3c), further proving that the RCDs are fixed at the interfacial Co-N-C to form a heterogeneous two-phase dot-sheet interface of as-prepared Co-N-C@CDs heterojunction (Fig. S3d). The elemental mapping results reveal a well-defined distribution of C, N, O, S and Co elements with the assembled Co-N-C@CDs, indicating the successful combination with a Co amount of 5.64%. As illustrated in Fig. 3d, the full-survey XPS spectra of Co-N-C@CDs present specific signals of surface chemical state. In general, alkali lignin with reactive −OH and –COOH is tend to bind with amino and sulfonic groups, resulting in the efficient N, S co-doping into aromatic molecules and stable linkage within the 0D-2D Co-N-C@CDs composite. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum is deconvoluted into C−C/C=C (285.1 eV), C−O (285.9 eV), and C=O (290.1 eV) respectively (Fig. 3e). The Co 2p can be separated into two types peaks of Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2 with Co2+ (786.2 and 803.1 eV) and Co3+ (782.4 and 798.5 eV), respectively (Fig. 3f). Fig. 3g exhibits the high-resolution N 1s that can be well-fitted into three peaks of pyrrolic N (399.3 eV), graphitic N (400.8 eV) and oxidized N (402.2 eV). For O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3h), three peaks are determined at 532.1, 532.8 and 534.1 eV referring to the C=O, C-bonded surface hydroxyls (C−OH), and C-O-C groups, respectively [41]. The S 2p pattern (Fig. 3i) of Co-N-C@CDs shows three peaks at approximately 167.9, 169.1, and 170.0 eV, which can be attributed to the presence of C-SO x -C sulfone bridges. In the as-prepared dot-sheet heterostructure, the N, S co-doping of CDs could strengthen the electroactive sites and interfacial conductivity. Moreover, the formation of N-O-C/C-O-C/C-O-S bonds is beneficial to interweaving CDs and Co-N-C that can expedite charge separation and migration.

From the Fig. 3j, the G band at 1583 cm−1 corresponds to sp 2 graphite carbon vibrations, while the D band at 1350 cm−1 is due to defected and disordered sp 3 carbon vibrations. The relative ratio of D to G strength (I D/I G) of GCDs (0.77) is slightly higher than that of RCDs (0.76), confirming the high graphitization of RCDs. The values of I D/I G increases gradually with introducing RCDs into Co-N-C, disclosing that the defects can be enhanced via the coupling of Co-N-C@CDs composites. The amorphous carbon structure of Co-N-C@CDs, as well as, their heteroatom functional groups and Co-coordinated sites are also confirmed by the XRD (Fig. S4a) and FTIR spectra (Fig. S4b). The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm present an obviously increased trend at low-pressure (P/P 0 < 0.1) and a hysteresis loop in high-pressure region (Fig. 3k), confirming the porous feature of Co-N-C@CDs [24]. In general, the tradeoff between accessible electrolyte ions (small pores), volumetric capacitance and mechanical strength (large pores) of the active electrode is needed for pore distribution. As depicted in the inset of Fig. 3k, the micropore-dominated porosity of Co-N-C@CDs ranging from ∼ 1 to 4 nm provides adequate channels of diffusing electrolyte ions with limited flooding. From Fig. 3i and Fig. S5, the Co-N-C@CDs offer a superior surface area (857.12 m2/g) and total pore volume (0.49 cm3 g−1) as compared with Co-N-C (589.38 m2/g and 0.35 cm3 g−1), reflecting the dot-sheet heterostructured superiority. It is well known that the large surface area with abundant micro- and mesopores favors the electrode ionic kinetics.

Due to the traits such as conductivity, nanoscale size, and electroactive sites, the intercalation of RCDs endows the lamellar Co-N-C nanosheets with improved electrochemical performance. In 6 M KOH electrolyte through a three-electrode system, therefore, we conducted the cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectrometry (EIS) measurements to investigate the activity of Co-N-C@CDs. As compared with Co-N-C electrode (Fig. S6a), the CV profile of Co-N-C@CDs (Fig. 4 a) has an appropriate working voltage window (∼0.5 V), large integrated area, and a major redox peak, implying the rapid ion transport and enhanced electrochemical properties [42]. The embedded RCDs enlarge the interlayer spacing of Co-N-C nanosheets to introduce adequate faradaic reaction that is responsible for the enhanced pseudocapacitive properties. Fig. 4b exhibits a stable potential plateau with no obvious voltage drop in the GCD curves of Co-N-C@CDs, indicating a battery-type dominated electrode behavior. The Co-N-C electrode embodies the main action of dual-layer capacitance storage (Fig. S6b), demonstrating the introduction of the redox sites into Co-N-C by the decoration of CDs. In Fig. 4c, the EIS curve of Co-N-C@CDs displays a smaller semicircle than that of Co-N-C (Fig. S6c), indicating a lower charge diffusion resistance of Co-N-C@CDs electrode [43]. As compared to the Co-N-C, the Co-N-C@CDs electrode has a smaller equivalent series resistances (Rs) and charge transfer resistance (Rct) (0.77 and 1.29 Ω vs. 9.68 and 37.86 Ω, respectively), affirming that the 0D-2D heterojunction of Co-N-C@CDs can establish the ideal geometric route and electronic structure to accelerate ion/electron transfer. The Co-N-C@CDs electrode has a specific capacitance of 328 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 (Fig. 4d), which is higher than that of Co-N-C (247 F g−1). The Co-N-C@CDs deliver a capacitance retention of ∼ 85.37% (280 F g−1) at 10 A/g, which is significantly higher than that of Co-N-C (∼40.49 %, 100 F g−1). The Co-N-C@CDs electrode has an excellent initial capacitance, high rate capability and conductivity with the addition of RCDs. The Co-N-C@CDs also exhibit a remarkable long-term cycling lifespan with the maintenance and coulomb efficiency of ∼ 86.39% and 99.76% respectively, over 10,000 charging-discharging cycles at cycles (Fig. 4e).

The fitting curve of the low-frequency region in EIS for Co-N-C@CDs is illustrated in Fig. 4f, which offer a low Warburg coefficient of 10.72 as compared with Co-N-C (11.93), proving a relatively faster diffusion rate in Co-N-C@CDs electrode. The relationship between redox current and scan rates can be estimated to assess the capacitance contribution and charge storage mechanism and of Co-N-C@CDs. Generally, the ideal slopes of reduction and oxidation reactions (b-value) are plotted to differentiate the diffusion-controlled (b, 0.5) and surface capacitive (b, 1.0) processes [44]. Fig. S7 shows that the b-values of oxidation and reduction reactions of Co-N-C@CDs are 0.56 and 0.52 respectively, indicating the mixed contribution of diffusion-controlled and capacitance-controlled processes. The percentage of capacitance contribution for Co-N-C@CDs at a sweep rate of 80 mV/s is calculated to be 53.95% (Fig. 4g). The capacitance contribution of Co-N-C@CDs is gradually enlarged from 32% to 82% with the increase of scan rate from 5 to 200 mV/s (Fig. 4h), which is higher than that of Co-N-C (Fig. S8). A capacitance-dominated behavior of Co-N-C@CDs refers to rapid ionic diffusion and excellent rate capability, which is conducive to subsequent CDI and ZIHCs performances [45].

To alleviate global accessible freshwater scarcity, the CDI technology is an emerging, energy-efficient, and cost-effective water desalination via the electric charge compensation and ionic adsorption/desorption capable of removing various ionic species [46,47]. It has been witnessed that the electrode materials, cell architectures, and associated mechanisms are of importance to boost the seawater desalination with superior salt adsorption capacity (SAC) and rapid salt adsorption rate (SAR) [48]. Herein, we demonstrate a high-performance seawater deionization system with an all-lignin dot-sheet heterostructured Co-N-C@CDs electrode. Fig. 5 a illustrates the schematic diagram of CDI device, wherein the ions move toward the electrode with opposite charges by applying an electrostatic forces. To investigate the capacitive performance of Co-N-C@CDs, a range of electrochemical tests are carried out to estimate the storage ability in 1 M NaCl solution. Fig. 5b displays a quasi-rectangular CV profile without the notable redox peak, indicating the reversible double-layered capacitance behavior of Co-N-C electrode in slat. With the increase of scan rate from 5 to 100 mV/s, the electrode exhibits a rapid current response with the amplification region. The equilateral triangle GCD curve of as-prepared Co-N-C@CDs electrode with negligible plateau illustrates the absence of redox reaction and ion intercalation during the reversible charging-discharging process (Fig. 5c). In the EIS of Fig. 5d, high-frequency magnification shows that the Co-N-C@CDs electrode has a relatively steep slope, low intrinsic resistance, and excellent ionic response. The porous Co-N-C@CDs with a low resistance provides a large specific surface area for rapid ion transfer which is ascribed to the presence of 0D-2D heterostructure and unique microchannel [49]. Meanwhile, the near-vertical lines with high slope at low frequency reveal the Warburg impedance originated from ion diffusion and capacitance properties [50]. The water desalination performance are evaluated by adjusting operating parameters in a symmetric CDI cell using the as-prepared Co-N-C@CDs electrodes at a constant pump flow rate of 20 mL min−1.

Fig. 5e shows a plot of SAC versus time of the NaCl solution with an initial concentration of 1000 mg L−1 at 1.2 V, which indicates that the adsorption capacity increases rapidly within 10 min and then gradually achieve equilibrium within 30 min. The hierarchical porous Co-N-C@CDs electrode showcases the maximum SAC of 33.64 mg g−1, highlighting the synergistic benefits from CDs-mediated microchannels within Co-N-C nanosheets. The unique 0D-2D dot-sheet heterojunction provides sufficient active sites and conduction paths, effectively enlarging the contact area and adsorption area between the electrode and salt ions. In addition, metal coordination has the capability to positively participate in electrochemical reactions during the seawater deionization, which in turn leads to the phenomena such as charge transfer and electrochemical sorption [51]. The reversible plot of conductivity in one cycle is shown in Fig. 5f. After 30 min, the solution conductivity gradually dropped down to an approximately constant value by applying a certain external voltage, proving that the ions are adsorbed on the Co-N-C@CDs electrode achieving dynamic equilibrium. When applying a reverse external voltage of 1.2 V, the output conductivity returned to its initial value rapidly via separating the ions from the surface of Co-N-C@CDs. Impressively, the solution conductivity within Co-N-C@CDs cell remains an initial value of 1870 µS cm−1 after 60 min, which indicates that the Co-N-C@CDs presents an excellent cycling stability in a reversible ion adsorption–desorption process. The SAC at the NaCl concentrations from 100 to 1000 mg L−1 under diverse voltages (1.0 − 1.2 V) are summarized in Fig. 5g. The results disclose that the desalination capacity and rate is increased with the elevation of working voltage, which is due to a high voltage capable of strengthening electrostatic force and driving ion insertion into the electrode [52]. Furthermore, the SAC values of Co-N-C@CDs showed an improvement with increasing NaCl concentrations from 100 to 1000 mg L−1, suggesting that hypersaline water is prone to amplify the electrical driving force for ion migration [53]. The CDI Ragone plots highlight the unique 0D-2D heterojunction featuring the superior SAC and SAR values of Co-N-C@CDs cell (Fig. 5h). Besides, the influence of working voltages on the CDI performance of Co-N-C@CDs are authenticated to deliver the optimal SAC (33.64 mg g−1) and SAR (4.3 mg g−1 min−1) in 1000 mg L−1 NaCl solution under 1.2 V. To make a direct comparison, we briefly summarize the SAC values of other reported carbon-based CDI electrodes as shown in Fig. 5i. Notably, the Co-N-C@CDs offer an excellent water desalination performance with high SAC value as compared with the recently reported CDI electrode materials (11.37 − 28.13 mg g−1) [54–64]. The superiority of using Co-N-C@CDs nanocomposites as CDI electrodes can be attributed to the following characteristics: (1) the intercalation of CDs into Co-N-C nanosheets assembles a 0D-2D anisotropic flower-like structure with an increased interlayer spacing, interconnected channels and hierarchical pores, thereby reducing ion adsorption energy barrier and accelerating charge migration kinetics; (2) the high crystallinity and ultralow size (<10 nm) of CDs can not only provide conductivity and active sites but also accelerate the diffusion of electrons and ions into the surface, thus improving the water desalination performance; (3) the N, S co-doping can expose the electroactive defect site and enhance the electron conductivity in as-prepared Co-N-C@CDs interface. Therefore, the assembled CDI devices with dot-sheet heterostructured Co-N-C@CDs electrode possess the superiorities of short charge diffusion pathway, good size dispersity, and high desalination rate (Fig. 5j).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of zinc-ion hybrid capacitors
ce:section.ce:para: ZIHCs combine Zn-ion batteries with supercapacitors to balance the energy and power densities of portable devices and electric automobiles. The extensive research has been carried out for developing carbon-based cathode to match the zinc anode that is a crucial issue of ZIHCs [45,65,66]. Herein, a self-made ZIHCs cells (Co-N-C@CDs//Zn) are assembled with Co-N-C@CDs electrode as a cathode, Zn foil (1 × 1 cm2) as the anode, and 1 M ZnSO4 as electrolyte. The schematic illustration of structural features, cell configuration, and energy storage mechanism of Co-N-C@CDs in ZIHCs are briefly proposed in Fig. 6 a. The energy storage of ZIHCs mainly depends on the quick adsorption and desorption of ions on the Co-N-C@CDs cathode surface during a charging/discharge cycle and the reversible plating and stripping of Zn2+ ions on the metallic anode [67]. The EIS pattern consisting of low-frequency line and high-frequency arc region is shown in Fig. 6b. In the high-frequency region, the half-circle represents the charge transfer resistance (Rct), while the linear part defines the resistance of zinc ion diffusion in the cathode (Zw) [68]. The fitting curve of ZIHCs cell shows a small charge transfer resistance (Rct = 21.56 Ω) and a low-frequency line with the high slope, demonstrating that Zn2+ has a high conductivity and powerful diffusion dynamics at the electrode–electrolyte interface. This is mainly due to the dot-sheet-assembled Co-N-C@CDs heterojunction producing hierarchical porous channels with micropores and mesopores for expediting ions transfer. A wide maximum operating voltage of 0.8−2.2 V are achieved in the CV profile of Fig. 6c. With a slight polarization at a voltage interval of 2.2 V, the operation of Co-N-C@CDs//Zn is suitable at the optimal voltage of 2.0 V. Hence the GCD curves of Co-N-C@CDs//Zn are measured at an operating voltage of 0−2 V. The GCD curves expand proportionally without deformation as the operating voltage increases, indicating effective utilization of both electrodes (Fig. 6d). The CV curves at diverse scan rates ranging from 5 to 100 mV/s are further performed at 0−2 V, suggesting that there are both electric double-layer capacitance and battery-type energy storage (Fig. 6e). The quasi-triangular shape of the GCD curves is maintained with the current density of 0.5−10 A g−1, which further demonstrates that ZIHCs has excellent capacitance characteristics and reversible charge–discharge cycling ability (Fig. 6f). To announce the energy storage mechanism of ZIHCs, the capacitive contribution (fast-kinetics) and the diffusion-controlled contribution (slow-kinetics) are decoupled from the CV profiles through varying sweep rates (Fig. 6g). With the amplifying of sweep rates from 5 to 100 mV/s, the capacitive contributions gradually heightened (44−78%) to exert the main double-layer capacitance behavior, approving the fast-kinetic advantage of the Co-N-C@CDs electrode. The ZIHCs deliver a high specific capacitance of 377.5 F g−1 at a current density of 0.5 A g−1, as well as, 154.5 F g−1 at a current density of 10 A g−1 (Fig. 6h). As depicted in Fig. 6i, the Co-N-C@CDs-installed ZIHCs exhibit a capacity retention of 85.4% even at a ultrahigh current density of 8 A g−1 after undergoing continuous 5000 charge–discharge cycles with a coulombic efficiency of 96.8% (a retention of > 80.23% with a coulombic efficiency of 97.5%, Fig. S9), illustrating the excellent long-cycling stability of rationally designed 0D-2D dot-sheet-heterostructured Co-N-C@CDs cathode. As summarized in Fig. 6j, the energy density of as-assembled ZIHCs is achieved as high as 209.72 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 500 W kg−1. Even at an ultrahigh power density of 10000 W kg−1, the energy density of ZIHCs is also maintained up to 91.39 Wh kg−1, which integrate the benefits of both ion batteries and supercapacitors. The performance of highly energy-dense Co-N-C@CDs cathode greatly exceeds many of the reported ZIHCs cell, such as Zn//MPC-2 ZIHC [69], Zn/Zn2+ [70], Zn//Zn and Zn//Cu [71], δ-MnO2@CAC [72], α-Co(OH)2/CoFe2O4 [14], rWC-6ZnN-12U [73], NiFe2O4/NiCo2O4/GO [74], and Zn-HS [75]. The hierarchically porous, ultrathin dot-sheet architecture with micro- and mesopores is propitious to boost the physical adsorption of Zn2+, while the introduction of interfacial pyrrolic-N, carboxyls and metallic sites ameliorates the chemical adsorption of Zn2+. The present work sheds substantial lights into the synergistic modification of all-lignin-based multiscale porous carbon composites with specific dot-sheet heterostructure, N, S co-doping, metal-coordination, and oxygen-containing functional groups for high-performance Zn-ion storage.

Overall, this work not only facilely introduces a novel 0D-2D carbon heterojunction into lignin valorization, but also provides a multifunctional electrode for CDI-motivated water desalination and high energy/power density ZIHCs. These achievements are mainly attributed to the following aspects: (1) the excellent electron donor–acceptor and hydrophilic properties of CDs enhance the electron transfer and stable improve the aqueous electrolyte diffusion (e.g., KOH, NaCl, ZnSO4); (2) the insertion of 0D CDs (∼8 nm) into the 2D lamellar porous Co-N-C nanosheets via supramolecular hydrogen bonding, π-π bridging and electrostatic interactions introduces ion-transport microchannels to the all-lignin heterojunction. At last, the optimized electronic and geometric structures, hierarchical micro-/mesopores, heteroatom functional species, and metallic electroactive spots are realized to maneuver the versatile electrochemical applications. Hence this work can not only pave a new avenue to lignin-first biorefining, but also provide a feasible strategy to explore multiscale-structured carbon composites in the area of water treatment and energy storage. The traditional utilization of biomass, such as combustion, gasification, and anaerobic digestion, is carbon–neutral. Of note, the valorization of waste lignin into carbon materials that fulfill carbon storage in a stable solid phase is a negative-emission technology [76]. To better comprehend the techno-economic feasibility of the proposed all-lignin-heterostructure procedure, the trade-off between the benefits of multifunctional Co-N-C@CDs electrode (e.g., CDI and ZIHCs) and the risk of economic parameters, energy input, capital, and operating costs should be taken into consideration in the scaling-up and practical operation. It is emphasized that the heterogeneous structure of native lignin is a crucial impediment to its large-scale utilization, which needs to be harnessed via the well-defined and cost-effective lignin fractionation. The in-depth insights into active sites, reaction kinetics and structural stability of lignin composites are still lacking, which is mainly rooted in the advanced characterization technologies to unravel its complex inter-linkages.

Harnessing lignin potential that occupies the technical barrier in lignocellulose biorefining is of great concern to advance the versatile functions of water purification and energy storage yet still challenging. Through a facile embedding of lignin-based CDs into lamellar Co-N-C nanosheets, we design a unique dot-sheet heterostructured supramolecular Co-N-C@CDs hybrids with the enlarged interlayer spacing, hierarchical porous networks and redox active sites. Taking advantage of a biphasic solvent fractionation, the multicolor light-emitting lignin-derived CDs with tunable size distribution (1.4−8.0 nm) and photophysical properties varying from blue, green to red are extracted to offer the selective sensing ability of metal-ion systems (e.g., Fe3+ and Ag+). Owing to the synergistic modification of all-lignin-based multiscale porous carbon composites with specific dot-sheet heterostructure, heteroatom co-doping, reactive metallic spots, and oxygen-containing functional groups, the as-prepared Co-N-C@CDs electrode delivers a high-performance electrochemical activity in CDI and ZIHCs. As a result, the all-lignin Co-N-C@CDs heterostructure-assembled CDI device exhibits a superior SAC of 33.64 mg g−1 and SAR of 4.3 mg g−1 min−1 in water desalination. Meanwhile, the installed ZIHCs (Zn//Co-N-C@CDs) offer a ultrahigh energy density of 209.72 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 500 W kg−1 (91.39 Wh kg−1 at 10000 W kg−1), as well as, an exceptional long-term cycling performance of 85.37% retention after 5000 charging-discharging cycles (at 8 A g−1) with an average coulombic efficiency of 96.8%. A dual charge storage rule with both ionic intercalation and reversible chemical adsorption in the aqueous electrolyte (e.g., KOH, NaCl and ZnSO4) are accomplished by the RCDs additive and metal-coordinated carbon nanosheets. We believe that our work can pave a broad route to fabricate multiscale-architecture of all-lignin electrodes that expand the functionality in water desalination and energy-storage technologies, servicing the carbon–neutral future with the emphasis of sustainable biomass-water-energy relationships.
